summary: Gruber criticizes the Software Update release notes UI in macOS Mojave 10.14 for being a textbook example of bad design. The release notes appear in a fixed-size, non-resizable sheet that shows only about 9 lines of text at a time. The text cannot be selected or copied, and URLs listed in the notes are not clickable, forcing users to retype them manually. He notes the irony that this tiny pane shows less text than an iPhone SE screen, despite Macs having large displays. He argues this level of UI negligence would be unacceptable in a student assignment, let alone in Apple's own system software. An update notes that the issue was eventually fixed in the very 10.14.4 update he was criticizing.
key_points: The release notes sheet cannot be resized, showing only ~9 lines despite lengthy update notes
Text is not selectable and URLs are not clickable, making the notes practically unusable for reference
Moving Software Update back to System Preferences from the App Store was the right call, but the execution of the release notes UI was badly neglected
The fixed pane displays less text than an iPhone SE screen, a damning comparison for a desktop OS
The lack of basic functionality — resizing, text selection, clickable links — reflects a quality standard unworthy of Apple
money_quotes: You see about 9 lines of text at a time, and there's nothing you can do about it.
Can't copy them, can't click them — the only way to actually open these URLs is to retype them manually.
Macs have displays ranging from large to giant, but these release notes are displayed in a fixed-size pane that shows less text than an iPhone SE.
The rigidity and clear lack of thoughtfulness makes it feel like a dialog box from some other operating system, like an old version of Windows.

If this sheet were part of a student's assignment in an intro to Mac programming class, a good teacher would send it back and explain how to make a sheet resizable, how to make text selectable (and thus copy-able), and how to make URLs clickable.
But this isn't a student assignment. It's MacOS system software.
key_insight: Apple's macOS Mojave release notes UI failed to meet the most basic standards of Mac software design — resizable windows, selectable text, clickable links — standards Apple itself established and should exemplify.
